---
deck: From status meeting to written update
audience: A team lead who runs a weekly status meeting. They are short on time and want fewer meetings, but worry the team will drift out of alignment without one.
register: presented
---

## Slide 1
layout: title
Title: From status meeting to written update
Subtitle: A proposal for a two-week trial
Notes: Open by naming the trade the team lead is weighing: time back against the risk of drift. The deck answers both.

## Slide 2
layout: section
Title: Where the hour goes
Notes: A short divider. Pause here so the room resets before the cost lands.

## Slide 3
layout: statement
Statement: The weekly status meeting costs the team six hours every week.
Notes: Six people, one hour. Say the number plainly and let it sit before moving on.

## Slide 4
layout: title-content
Title: What the meeting does, and does not, do well
Body:
- It surfaces blockers fast, while everyone is in the room.
- It gives quieter teammates a fixed moment to be heard.
- It spends the same hour whether the week was eventful or quiet.
- It leaves no record anyone can search the following month.
Notes: Concede the real strengths first. The proposal keeps what works and fixes what does not.

## Slide 5
layout: two-column
Title: Two ways to spend the same hour
Left: The meeting. Everyone stops at the same time. Updates are spoken once and gone. The slowest item sets the pace for all six people.
Right: The written update. Each person writes for ten minutes when it suits them. Blockers are flagged in the document and answered in a thread. The record stays searchable.
Visual: A simple two-panel diagram, one panel per column, the written-update panel carrying the brand accent colour.
Notes: This is the heart of the argument. Walk the room across the comparison slowly.

## Slide 6
layout: quote
Quote: I skip half of what I say in standup because it does not apply to most of the room.
Attribution: A teammate, in last month's retro
Notes: A real voice from inside the team carries more weight here than another bullet would.
